title: Top 20 Social Ills from Private Equity and Financialization

items.name: Leveraged buyout debt loading and productive asset stripping
items.description: The core PE playbook: acquire a company using its own assets as collateral, load it with debt to pay dividends, extract fees at every stage, and leave the hollowed entity to survive or die under the debt burden. Gains are privatized; losses are socialized through bankruptcy, job loss, and pension default.

items.name: Destruction of healthcare through PE acquisition
items.description: Private equity has systematically acquired hospitals, nursing homes, physician practices, emergency staffing companies, and dental chains, then cut staffing, increased patient loads, and extracted fees. Research consistently shows PE-owned healthcare facilities have worse patient outcomes and higher mortality.

items.name: Gutting of local retail and the strip-mall apocalypse
items.description: PE-driven roll-ups and debt-loaded acquisitions accelerated the death of retail chains, destroying not just jobs but the commercial anchors of communities. The empty storefronts are monuments to extraction.

items.name: Housing financialization and the landlord-ification of America
items.description: After 2008, PE firms and institutional investors bought hundreds of thousands of single-family homes at distressed prices, converting homeownership stock into rental stock. The foreclosure crisis became a business model, permanently pricing out the families whose foreclosures created the inventory.

items.name: Destruction of local journalism via hedge fund acquisition
items.description: Hedge funds and PE firms have acquired newspaper chains specifically to extract remaining cash flows while cutting newsrooms to skeleton crews. The firm does not want a newspaper; it wants the real estate and the cash flow during the wind-down.

items.name: Private equity roll-ups creating stealth monopolies
items.description: PE firms acquire dozens of small businesses in a single sector (veterinary clinics, dermatology practices, car washes, funeral homes), consolidate them, raise prices, and cut quality — all below the threshold that triggers antitrust review. The consumer experiences a monopoly without any single acquisition being large enough to attract regulatory attention.

items.name: Systematic wage and benefit suppression post-acquisition
items.description: PE-owned companies are significantly more likely to cut wages, reduce benefits, eliminate pensions, and increase worker turnover than comparable non-PE companies. The cost savings that fund investor returns come directly from worker compensation.

items.name: Pension destruction and retirement insecurity
items.description: PE-acquired companies have a documented pattern of underfunding pensions, converting defined-benefit to defined-contribution plans, and in bankruptcy dumping pension obligations onto the PBGC and ultimately taxpayers. Workers who spent decades earning a pension find it gutted.

items.name: Gig economy and workforce fissuring
items.description: Financialization drove the reclassification of employees as independent contractors, outsourcing of core functions to staffing agencies, and the general fissuring of the workplace. The goal is to disconnect the worker from the entity that profits from their labor, eliminating obligations while retaining control.

items.name: Public sector austerity as financialization dividend
items.description: Municipal debt restructuring, privatization of public services (water, parking, prisons), and the push to run government like a business are financialization's public sector expression. Chicago leasing its parking meters to Morgan Stanley for 75 years for a lump sum spent in three years is the paradigmatic case.

items.name: Stock buyback epidemic and death of corporate reinvestment
items.description: Financialization reoriented corporate purpose from producing goods and services to producing shareholder returns. Stock buybacks, illegal before 1982, now consume more capital than R&D and capital expenditure combined at many large firms. Companies are eating themselves to boost share prices.

items.name: Short-termism and destruction of long-range planning
items.description: Quarterly earnings pressure, activist investor campaigns, and the constant threat of hostile takeover have made long-term investment in R&D, workforce development, and infrastructure economically irrational for public company CEOs. The time horizon of American capitalism has collapsed from decades to quarters.

items.name: Financialization of higher education and the student debt crisis
items.description: Treating education as a financial product to be leveraged created the 1.7 trillion dollar student debt overhang. For-profit colleges, often PE-owned, were the most predatory expression, but the broader financialization of higher education affected the entire sector.

items.name: Private equity capture of government contracting
items.description: PE-owned firms are now dominant in defense contracting, IT services, and government outsourcing, creating a loop where public money flows to private equity through contracts, firms cut costs and quality to extract returns, and the public gets degraded services while PE partners get carried interest taxed at capital gains rates.

items.name: Systemic risk concentration and too-big-to-fail entrenchment
items.description: Financialization created institutions so large and interconnected that their failure threatens the entire system, giving them permanent implicit government backing. The 2008 bailout demonstrated socialized downside risk with privatized upside. This has been intensified, not fixed.

items.name: Wealth inequality acceleration
items.description: Financialization is the primary mechanism through which wealth has concentrated since 1980. The share of GDP going to the financial sector roughly doubled from approximately 4% to approximately 8% while median wages stagnated. Every dollar of financial sector rent is a dollar not going to wages, infrastructure, or productive investment.

items.name: Carried interest loophole and tax system corruption
items.description: PE managers pay capital gains rates on their income rather than ordinary income rates, a policy with no economic justification that survives solely because of lobbying power. The carried interest loophole is a litmus test for whether the political system serves concentrated wealth or the public interest.

items.name: Democratic capture and regulatory revolving door
items.description: The financial sector spends more on lobbying than any other industry, and the revolving door between Wall Street, the Treasury, the Fed, and the SEC creates structural regulatory capture. The people writing the rules and the people being regulated are the same social class.

items.name: Commodification of essential services
items.description: Financialization has turned housing, healthcare, education, water, prisons, and elder care into asset classes optimized for yield rather than for the people they serve. When a nursing home is a portfolio company rather than a place where your mother lives, the optimization function changes.

items.name: Erosion of the social contract and legitimacy of capitalism itself
items.description: The cumulative effect of financialization is a population that correctly perceives the economic system is rigged — gains flow upward, losses flow downward, and the rules are written by the beneficiaries. This accurate perception is the single greatest threat to democratic capitalism's legitimacy and feeds populist radicalization on both left and right.

structural_observation: Financialization is the mechanism that connects many ills on other lists. Offshoring was driven by it. Tech platforms were funded by it. The housing crisis is a product of it. Healthcare collapse accelerates through it. It is the operating system beneath the other disruptions. The extraction is the product.
